content.text: Server-Sent Events (SSE) push a continuous stream of text events from server to browser over a single, long-lived HTTP connection. For AI agent backends, SSE is the dominant protocol for displaying LLM tokens incrementally, relaying tool-call progress in real time, and delivering reasoning traces without requiring the client to poll or open a bidirectional socket.
SSE is a W3C specification that defines a simple wire format for server-to-client event push over ordinary HTTP. The server responds with
and keeps the connection open, emitting newline-delimited text blocks. Each block is one event. The browser-native
API subscribes to these events and fires callbacks for each one. Non-browser clients — Python scripts, other microservices — consume SSE using any HTTP client that can read a streaming body, such as
The core wire format is deliberately minimal: each event is one or more field lines followed by a blank line. The
field carries the payload (typically JSON). The optional
field names the event type. The optional
field marks a sequence number that the browser sends back as
field advises the client how long to wait before reconnecting. A line starting with
is a comment and is ignored by clients — useful for keepalive pings.

from fastapi import FastAPI
from fastapi.responses import StreamingResponse
import asyncio
import json

app = FastAPI()

async def token_generator(prompt: str):
    """Async generator that yields SSE-formatted text events."""
    tokens = prompt.split()  # placeholder; replace with real LLM call
    event_id = 0
    for token in tokens:
        event_id += 1
        payload = json.dumps({"delta": token + " ", "index": event_id})
        # Each SSE event: optional id line, data line, blank line terminator
        yield f"id: {event_id}\ndata: {payload}\n\n"
        await asyncio.sleep(0.05)  # simulate per-token latency
    # Terminal event signals end of stream
    yield "event: done\ndata: [DONE]\n\n"

@app.get("/stream")
async def stream_endpoint(prompt: str = "Hello agent world"):
    return StreamingResponse(
        token_generator(prompt),
        media_type="text/event-stream",
        headers={
            "Cache-Control": "no-cache",
            "Connection": "keep-alive",
            "X-Accel-Buffering": "no",  # disable nginx buffering
        },
    )

Modern LLM APIs stream their output token by token. Without streaming, the user stares at a blank screen for the entire generation time — often 5 to 30 seconds for long answers. SSE bridges the LLM's internal streaming to the browser with minimal code. It uses plain HTTP, so it traverses corporate proxies, CDNs, and load balancers that would otherwise block raw WebSocket upgrades. Because the browser handles reconnection automatically, the client code is far simpler than a comparable WebSocket implementation.

For agentic backends, SSE is even more valuable: an agent may take dozens of tool-call cycles before producing a final answer. Without a streaming channel, the user has no feedback. With SSE, the server can emit named events for each phase —
— giving the UI rich, granular progress information on a single connection opened by a single GET request.
— a single GET request opens the stream; no handshake or protocol upgrade needed
— traverses any proxy, CDN, or load balancer that handles regular HTTP without special configuration
to resume from where it left off
fields let the client register separate listeners for different event types without manual routing
— no framing library, no binary protocol, no state machine; the browser parses the text format natively
— credentials travel in the initial HTTP request (cookies, Bearer tokens), not in a separate handshake frame
— the client cannot push data back on the same connection; input requires a separate HTTP POST or WebSocket
— binary payloads must be base64-encoded or sent through a parallel channel
— browsers historically allow ~6 concurrent connections per origin; many open SSE streams exhaust this budget (HTTP/2 multiplexing eliminates the problem but requires TLS)
) buffer the response body until the connection closes, destroying the streaming effect
— SSE has no flow-control mechanism; a slow client simply falls behind and may miss events if the server does not buffer them
Every SSE response body is a UTF-8 text stream. Events are delimited by a blank line. The four defined field names are
. Any unrecognised field name is silently ignored by the parser. A single event may span multiple
lines; the client joins them with newline characters before dispatching. Multi-line data payloads require one

# Minimal event (most common)
data: {"delta": "Hello"}

# Named event with sequence id
event: tool_call
id: 42
data: {"tool": "search", "query": "SSE specification"}

# Multi-line data payload (lines are joined with \n)
data: first line
data: second line

# Comment line - keepalive; ignored by EventSource
: keepalive ping

# Reconnection advice - browser waits 3 s before retry
retry: 3000

# Terminal sentinel (OpenAI / Anthropic convention)
data: [DONE]
The client issues a GET request with the
). The server holds the connection open and flushes bytes as events arrive. The connection ends when the server closes it (by returning from the generator) or the client disconnects. On disconnect,
waits for the retry interval (default 3 seconds) and re-opens the connection, sending a
header equal to the last received
Server emits: event blocks flushed immediately as bytes become available
Stream ends: server generator returns; client reconnects after retry delay
FastAPI streaming response with named events

from fastapi import FastAPI
from fastapi.responses import StreamingResponse
import asyncio, json

app = FastAPI()

# Named events let the client register targeted listeners
# instead of routing inside one generic onmessage handler.

async def agent_event_stream(run_id: str):
    """Emit multiple named event types for an agent run."""
    # 1. Thinking event (agent reasoning trace)
    yield f"event: thinking\ndata: {json.dumps({'text': 'Analysing the request...'})}\n\n"
    await asyncio.sleep(0.2)

    # 2. Tool call event
    yield (
        f"event: tool_call\n"
        f"id: 1\n"
        f"data: {json.dumps({'tool': 'search', 'query': 'agentic AI SSE'})}\n\n"
    )
    await asyncio.sleep(0.5)

    # 3. Tool result event
    yield (
        f"event: tool_result\n"
        f"id: 2\n"
        f"data: {json.dumps({'result': 'SSE is a W3C standard...'})}\n\n"
    )
    await asyncio.sleep(0.1)

    # 4. Token stream for final answer
    for word in ["SSE", " enables", " real-time", " streaming."]:
        yield f"event: token\ndata: {json.dumps({'delta': word})}\n\n"
        await asyncio.sleep(0.05)

    # 5. Terminal event
    yield f"event: done\ndata: {json.dumps({'run_id': run_id})}\n\n"

@app.get("/agent/{run_id}/stream")
async def stream_run(run_id: str):
    return StreamingResponse(
        agent_event_stream(run_id),
        media_type="text/event-stream",
        headers={"Cache-Control": "no-cache", "X-Accel-Buffering": "no"},
    )
Consuming SSE as a non-browser client
Python agent backends often need to forward an SSE stream from one service to another, or consume an LLM's SSE stream inside a tool. The
library's async streaming mode makes this straightforward. The key insight is that each SSE event spans multiple lines separated by newlines, terminated by a blank line.

import asyncio
import httpx
import json

async def consume_sse(url: str):
    """Consume an SSE stream using httpx; works outside the browser."""
    accumulated = []
    async with httpx.AsyncClient(timeout=None) as client:
        async with client.stream("GET", url) as resp:
            resp.raise_for_status()
            async for raw_line in resp.aiter_lines():
                line = raw_line.strip()
                if not line or line.startswith(":"):
                    # blank line = event boundary; comment lines start with ':'
                    continue
                if line.startswith("data: "):
                    data = line[6:]
                    if data == "[DONE]":
                        break
                    chunk = json.loads(data)
                    accumulated.append(chunk.get("delta", ""))
                    print(chunk["delta"], end="", flush=True)
    return "".join(accumulated)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    result = asyncio.run(consume_sse("http://localhost:8000/stream?prompt=Hello"))
    print(f"\nFull response: {result}")
— render partial answers as they arrive; SSE is the standard Anthropic and OpenAI streaming transport
— emit named events for each reasoning step, tool call, and result without polling
— stream server-side log lines to a browser terminal in real time
— push alerts, pipeline status updates, or async job completions to a web UI
when the client must also send frequent messages back on the same channel — use WebSockets instead
for binary media (images, audio buffers) — prefer WebSockets or separate pre-signed URLs
Step 1: set up the FastAPI streaming endpoint
The only requirement is that the route handler returns a
and an async generator body. FastAPI sets
automatically and flushes bytes to the client as the generator yields. The three critical response headers are:
Step 2: add keepalive heartbeats for long-running streams

LLM calls that take many seconds risk having the connection dropped by load balancers or CDNs with idle-connection timeouts (often 30 to 60 seconds). The fix is to emit SSE comment lines periodically. A comment is a line beginning with

followed by any text; the browser ignores it but it flushes bytes through the TCP stack and resets proxy idle timers. The wrapper generator below can be layered around any inner generator without modifying it.

import asyncio, json
from fastapi import FastAPI
from fastapi.responses import StreamingResponse

app = FastAPI()

HEARTBEAT_INTERVAL = 15  # seconds

async def with_heartbeat(inner_gen, interval: float = HEARTBEAT_INTERVAL):
    """Wrap any async generator with periodic keepalive comment lines."""
    queue: asyncio.Queue = asyncio.Queue()

    async def drain():
        async for chunk in inner_gen:
            await queue.put(chunk)
        await queue.put(None)  # sentinel

    async def pump():
        drain_task = asyncio.create_task(drain())
        while True:
            try:
                item = await asyncio.wait_for(queue.get(), timeout=interval)
                if item is None:
                    drain_task.cancel()
                    return
                yield item
            except asyncio.TimeoutError:
                yield ": keepalive\n\n"

    async for chunk in pump():
        yield chunk

async def slow_llm_stream():
    await asyncio.sleep(20)  # simulate slow model
    yield f"data: {json.dumps({'delta': 'final answer'})}\n\n"
    yield "event: done\ndata: [DONE]\n\n"

@app.get("/slow-stream")
async def slow_endpoint():
    return StreamingResponse(
        with_heartbeat(slow_llm_stream()),
        media_type="text/event-stream",
        headers={"Cache-Control": "no-cache", "X-Accel-Buffering": "no"},
    )
Step 3: make streams resumable with Last-Event-ID
If the browser disconnects mid-stream — network hiccup, tab backgrounded, phone locked — it will automatically reconnect and send the
it received. The server should log events to a durable store (Redis, Postgres) and replay events with id greater than
on reconnect. For LLM token streams the replay is rarely needed (re-running the model is expensive), but for tool-call status feeds and notification channels it is essential for correctness.

import asyncio, json
from fastapi import FastAPI, Request
from fastapi.responses import StreamingResponse

app = FastAPI()

# Simulated event log - in production this comes from a DB or cache
EVENT_LOG = [
    {"id": i, "data": {"delta": f"token_{i}"}}
    for i in range(1, 51)
]

async def resumable_stream(last_event_id: int | None):
    """Resume from last_event_id if the client reconnects."""
    start = last_event_id + 1 if last_event_id is not None else 1
    for event in EVENT_LOG:
        if event["id"] < start:
            continue  # skip already-delivered events
        payload = json.dumps(event["data"])
        yield f"id: {event['id']}\ndata: {payload}\n\n"
        await asyncio.sleep(0.05)
    yield f"event: done\ndata: [DONE]\n\n"

@app.get("/resumable-stream")
async def resumable_endpoint(request: Request):
    # Browser sends Last-Event-ID header on reconnect
    raw = request.headers.get("Last-Event-ID")
    last_id = int(raw) if raw and raw.isdigit() else None
    return StreamingResponse(
        resumable_stream(last_id),
        media_type="text/event-stream",
        headers={"Cache-Control": "no-cache", "X-Accel-Buffering": "no"},
    )
The following snippet shows how to test an SSE endpoint with
and how to simulate a reconnect with
flag disables curl's output buffering so events print immediately.

# Run the server
uvicorn app:app --reload

# Consume the stream with curl (--no-buffer / -N)
curl -N "http://localhost:8000/stream?prompt=Hello+agent"

# Expected output (each data line arrives ~50ms apart):
# id: 1
# data: {"delta": "Hello ", "index": 1}
#
# id: 2
# data: {"delta": "agent ", "index": 2}
#
# event: done
# data: [DONE]
#

# Simulate reconnect with Last-Event-ID header:
curl -N -H "Last-Event-ID: 1" "http://localhost:8000/resumable-stream"
# Server will skip event id=1 and start from id=2
A production AI assistant opens one SSE connection per conversation turn. When the user submits a message, the frontend POSTs the message to
in the response. It then opens
. The agent backend emits named events in sequence: first a
event with the chain-of-thought reasoning; then one or more
events as the model invokes search, code execution, or database lookups;
events when each tool returns; and finally a stream of
events for the final user-facing answer. A

The UI renders each event type differently: reasoning is shown in a collapsible trace panel, tool calls appear as animated cards, and tokens accumulate character-by-character in the message bubble. The entire conversation flow requires exactly two HTTP request types — one POST to start a run, one GET to stream its output — and no persistent client-side connections to manage between turns.
— each SSE event must end with two newlines (
); a single newline does not flush the event to listeners
responses unless told not to; always set
— the default nginx config buffers upstream responses; SSE requires
— gzip buffers output to achieve compression; disable it for SSE locations or the stream will not flow until the buffer fills
— load balancers often drop connections idle for 30 to 60 seconds; a model taking 90 seconds without yielding will kill the stream mid-generation
— Flask and Django WSGI apps cannot truly stream; use ASGI (FastAPI, Starlette, Quart) with async generators for genuine non-blocking streaming
— if the server ignores the header, clients re-receive duplicate data or miss events after a disconnect
line; version the schema with a
field so clients can handle format changes without breaking
fields for any stream where replay on reconnect matters; omit them for ephemeral single-use token streams
) so clients know when to close cleanly; never rely on the connection dropping
Send comment keepalive lines every 15 to 20 seconds for long-running generations to prevent proxy idle timeouts
Set read timeouts on the upstream LLM call independently of the SSE connection lifetime; a stalled LLM call should raise an error event, not silently hang
Log SSE endpoint metrics (connection duration, bytes sent, events emitted) — these are invisible to standard request/response APMs which only see the initial request
and with a browser DevTools Network tab filtered to
; the browser tool shows each parsed event as a named row

Each open SSE connection holds one server-side coroutine and one TCP socket. With an async framework like FastAPI running on uvicorn, a single Python process can hold thousands of concurrent SSE connections because awaiting on an LLM stream yields the event loop thread — no threads or processes are consumed while waiting for the next token. The dominant resource is TCP socket descriptors (the

For horizontal scaling, SSE connections are sticky — they are point-to-point between one client and one server process. If an agent run executes on server A, the SSE stream must be served by server A (or proxied to it). Two patterns manage this at scale: (1) use a message bus such as Redis Pub/Sub to fan out events from the agent worker to any server process serving the stream, or (2) use sticky load-balancer routing (IP hash, session cookie) to ensure the same client always reaches the same worker.
— only ASGI servers (uvicorn, hypercorn) support concurrent SSE at scale; WSGI blocks a thread per connection
— eliminates the browser 6-connection-per-origin limit; configure nginx or Caddy with HTTP/2 and a valid TLS certificate
— the agent worker publishes events to a Redis channel; each SSE handler subscribes and forwards to its client, decoupling execution from delivery
— cap SSE connections per user with a semaphore or connection-count header check to prevent resource exhaustion
— never accumulate the full LLM response before streaming; yield each chunk immediately to maintain perceived latency
StreamingResponse with async generator; built-in ASGI
async with client.stream() for non-browser consumption
Native W3C API; auto-reconnects; no library needed
Adds POST support and retry control missing from native EventSource
Runs FastAPI; supports HTTP/1.1 and HTTP/2 streaming
Set proxy_buffering off and proxy_read_timeout for SSE routes
Distributes events across multiple SSE server instances
— SSE opens with a GET request; verify credentials via cookie (automatic for same-origin) or a signed query-string token; never expose unauthenticated SSE endpoints
Scope streams to the requesting user
belongs to the authenticated user before streaming; SSE connections are long-lived and an authorization check at connection open is the right moment
— each SSE connection ties up a server resource; apply rate limits on the stream endpoint (e.g., max 5 concurrent streams per API key) to prevent denial-of-service
Do not leak internal errors in stream payloads
with a safe message and close the stream; log the full error server-side with a correlation id
— close idle or very long-running connections after a configurable timeout (e.g., 10 minutes) to prevent resource leaks from forgotten browser tabs
— treat the header as untrusted user input; parse it strictly (integer only, within expected range) before replaying events from storage

: the server is the broadcaster and each client is a receiver tuned to one channel. The broadcaster transmits messages in sequence; receivers hear them as they arrive. If a receiver loses signal (network drop), it tunes back in and catches up from where it left off — provided the station keeps a short recording of recent broadcasts. Unlike a phone call (WebSockets), the receiver cannot talk back on the same channel; it uses a different medium (an HTTP POST) to send a message to the station.
SSE = one-way, server-to-client text event stream delivered over a single HTTP GET connection
Format events as one or more field lines (
Emit keepalive comment lines every 15 to 20 seconds to prevent proxy idle-timeout disconnects
field) to carry multiple logical message types on one stream without client-side routing logic
values and replay missed events when the client sends
Choose WebSockets only when true bidirectional streaming on the same connection is required; SSE is simpler for the token-streaming use case that dominates LLM applications
